In the time before time, the sky was a heavy, unbroken slate of grey stone that pressed low against the earth, suffocating the young mountains. The people walked with bent backs, unable to see beyond the immediate horizon. Among them lived Elara, a weaver who spun threads not from wool, but from the unspoken dreams of sleepers. 

One night, Elara dreamed of a vast, open blue expanse. Waking with a fierce longing, she took her sharpest needle, forged from a fallen star-shard, and began to climb the highest peak, Mount Orogen. The climb took years, her back straightening with every step upward against the crushing weight. When she finally reached the summit, the stone sky was just inches above her head.

With a cry of defiance, Elara thrust her needle upward. It pierced the stone, and a single, brilliant beam of golden light shot through the hole. Encouraged, she stabbed again and again, frantically piercing the grey barrier. With every puncture, the heavy stone cracked and retreated higher, becoming the dark veil of night, while the holes she made remained as the stars, leaking the eternal light of the universe beyond. Her final, greatest thrust shattered a massive piece of the stone, creating the moon, a reminder of the barrier that once held them down.

Long ago, before the mountains had names and the rivers knew their paths, the Sky and the Sea were lovers who could never touch. The Sky, vast and lonely, wept tears of silver light because he could not hold the Sea. These tears fell into the dark waters, sinking to the crushing depths where no light had ever been.

The Sea, moved by her lover’s sorrow, gathered the silver tears in her cold, fluid hands. She could not return them to the heights, so she began to sing a song of pressure and time. For eons, she polished the tears against the forgotten stones of the deep, compressing the sorrow into something solid and bright.

One night, when the darkness was absolute, the Sea exhaled a mighty breath. From the waves rose a sphere of pure, cold light—the Moon. It was the Sky’s own tears, returned to him, now solid enough to hang in his embrace.

Now, the Sea reaches for the Moon every night in the form of tides, trying to kiss the gift she made, and the Sky holds it close, a silent promise that even in the vast distance, their love illuminates the dark.
